Extract Text: Many species of plant-eating megafauna such as mammoths and mastodons died out in North America at the end of the last ice age (roughly 10,000 BC), and some scholars claim that they were eradicated primarily by human hunters who arrived on the continent beginning around 14,000 BC. However, others think that the cause was a concurrent change in plant distribution, generally believed to be due to warming of the planet at the end of the ice age.
Question Text: Which of the following, if found correct, would most pertain to the relative tenability of the two hypotheses?
Choice A: The analysis of megafauna bones indicates declining nutrition beginning around 10,000 BC.
Choice B: The population of meat-eating animals declined at the same rate as that of plant-eating megafauna.
Choice C: The relative availability of the various edible plant species at the end of the ice age affected the vegetable diet of human populations.
Choice D: Human populations were evenly distributed across North America by 12,000 BC.
Choice E: Mammoths and mastodons were able to tolerate the warmer temperatures at the end of the ice age.
Rationale:
The analysis of megafauna bones indicates declining nutrition beginning around 10,000 BC.

This finding directly supports the hypothesis that human hunting may have contributed to the decline of megafauna, as it implies that nutritional stress experienced by these animals could have made them more vulnerable to hunting pressures at that time. A) The analysis of megafauna bones indicates declining nutrition beginning around 10,000 BC. This choice directly relates to the decline of megafauna and supports the idea that nutrition played a critical role in their extinction. If megafauna were experiencing declining nutrition, this would make them more susceptible to other stressors, including human hunting, thereby reinforcing the argument of human influence during this period. B) The population of meat-eating animals declined at the same rate as that of plant-eating megafauna. While this choice discusses the decline of meat-eating animals, it does not provide insight into the specific causes of megafauna extinction. A parallel decline does not directly indicate whether human hunting or environmental changes were responsible, making it less relevant to the hypotheses in question. C) The relative availability of the various edible plant species at the end of the ice age affected the vegetable diet of human populations. This option focuses on human dietary changes rather than the decline of megafauna. While it may highlight shifts in human behavior, it does not address the direct impacts of plant distribution changes or human hunting on the megafauna themselves, thus diminishing its relevance to the hypotheses. D) Human populations were evenly distributed across North America by 12,000 BC. This choice pertains to human distribution and does not provide evidence concerning the megafauna's decline. The distribution of human populations does not clarify whether their hunting practices significantly affected megafauna extinction or if environmental changes played a larger role. E) Mammoths and mastodons were able to tolerate the warmer temperatures at the end of the ice age. This statement suggests resilience in megafauna to climate change, which counters the idea that warming alone led to their extinction. If they could indeed adapt to the warmer climate, it weakens the argument for environmental change as the primary factor in their decline. Conclusion The analysis of megafauna bones indicating declining nutrition around 10,000 BC provides crucial evidence that supports the hypothesis of human hunting impacting these species. While other choices address related factors, they do not directly pertain to the core question of the relative tenability of the hypotheses regarding megafauna extinction. Understanding the interplay between human activity and environmental changes is vital for interpreting the extinction of these ancient species.
